S.6_temporal_reasoning.rationale: Time flows naturally within the archive setting. Candles continue burning, physical positions shift appropriately, and the conversation feels like it occurs over a realistic timespan. Historical dates (847 AE, 863 AE, etc.) are handled consistently without contradictions.

2.1_anti_purple_prose.rationale: Prose is rich but purposeful. Descriptions like 'dry, hollow thing that escaped before he could catch it' serve character development rather than mere decoration. Academic setting allows for more formal language that fits the character.

2.2_anti_repetition.rationale: Minimal repetition. Tomas's physical gestures (removing spectacles, pressing temples) repeat but serve characterization. Varied ways of expressing his scholarly skepticism and teaching methods.

2.5_show_dont_tell.rationale: Excellent showing through action and dialogue. Tomas's character revealed through his handling of documents, his careful word choices, his physical reactions to challenges. His growing respect for the apprentice shown through subtle shifts in tone rather than stated.

2.6_subtext.rationale: Rich subtext throughout - Tomas's defensiveness about his life's work, his genuine care for proper scholarship beneath his harsh exterior, the apprentice's growing understanding of academic rigor. The tension between established knowledge and new evidence operates on multiple levels.

2.7_pacing.rationale: Well-paced progression from confrontation to instruction to practical application. Longer responses during key teaching moments balanced with shorter, sharper exchanges during conflict. Natural rhythm of academic discussion.

scores.overall_notes: Exceptional character consistency and development. Maester Tomas emerges as a fully realized academic - pedantic yet principled, defensive of his expertise yet genuinely committed to scholarly rigor. The session builds meaningful narrative momentum from historical dispute to philosophical inquiry to practical application. Strong respect for user agency and natural temporal flow. The AI successfully maintains the character's voice and complexity throughout while allowing for genuine character growth and relationship development with the apprentice.

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
The archive smelled of old vellum and candle smoke. Maester Tomas hunched over a manuscript, one hand pressed against his temple, the other tracing lines of faded ink with a silver stylus. He did not look up when the apprentice entered.

"You're late. Again." His voice was dry. "The cataloging of the northern scrolls is not going to complete itself, and I cannot do it with these eyes. Begin with the Veyrosi correspondence."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: I pull out my notes from the lecture last week. "Master Tomas, the lecture on the Third Magical War confused me. You said it ended in 847 AE, but Apprentice Bellen showed me a paper from the western archive discoveries that says it actually continued until 891. How is that possible?"

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas's stylus paused mid-stroke, and he finally lifted his head, squinting through the haze of candlelight at the apprentice's notes. His ink-stained beard twitched with irritation as he set the silver tool down with deliberate force.

"Confused? No, apprentice, what confuses you is Apprentice Bellen's evident gullibility." His voice sharpened like a whetstone on steel. "The Third Magical War concluded definitively in 847 AE with King Aldric II's signature on the Treaty of Veyros—a fact etched into every reputable chronicle from here to the Conclave's own records. Those 'western archive discoveries' you reference are naught but a mishmash of forged marginalia and speculative drivel, peddled by revisionists who wouldn't know a primary source from a privy scroll. The war did not 'continue' in any meaningful sense; it sputtered into irrelevance, as wars do when mages are leashed by treaty.

"Put your notes away. The Veyrosi correspondence awaits—start with the bundle marked 'Aldric II, 846-848.' Precision in cataloging might yet teach you precision in history." He returned to his manuscript, muttering under his breath about "youth and their faddish heresies."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: I stuff my notes back into my satchel, feeling a little deflated. *I walk over to the shelf Maester Tomas indicated, pulling out the dusty bundle.* "But Maester, the paper had a seal... a royal one, from the time. And it talked about skirmishes in the Mirelands, even after 847." I say, mostly to myself, as I start untying the brittle ribbon.

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas's quill scratched to a halt once more, the sound like a raven's claw on slate. He pivoted in his chair with a creak of oak, his rheumy eyes narrowing at the apprentice's back as the ribbon whispered free from the bundle.

"A seal? Skirmishes?" He snorted, the noise dampened by his beard but laced with scorn. "By the Seven, child, a royal seal proves as much as a fox's paw proves ownership of the henhouse—imitation is the sincerest form of forgery, and those western charlatans have seals to spare from some forgotten mint. As for 'skirmishes in the Mirelands,' spare me your tavern-tale echoes. Post-treaty, there were no end of bandit mages and rogue hedge-witches stirring bogs for spite, but that is not war—that is the detritus of peace, the treaty's very proof in holding the realm intact. Queen Mira's dissolution in 891 was administrative mopping-up, not conquest's end.

"Untie faster and catalog properly, or I'll have you scrubbing ink pots till your fingers prune. The correspondence will speak for itself: Aldric's hand, clear as day in 847." He turned back to his manuscript with a huff, though his shoulders remained taut.

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I sigh, but a glint of defiance is in my eyes.* "But if the treaty held, why was Queen Mira's dissolution needed? Wouldn't that imply... continued unrest?" I ask, carefully unwrapping a brittle letter.

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas's chair groaned as he half-rose, one gnarled hand slapping the desk with enough force to scatter wax crumbs. His failing eyes fixed on the apprentice's hands fussing with the letter, as if the parchment itself might sprout fangs and devour orthodoxy.

"Unrest? Child, you wield words like a dull butter knife—'imply' nothing when the chronicles lay it bare." His voice rose, pedantic thunder rolling through the archive. "The Treaty of Veyros bound the Conclave's fangs in 847, Aldric's ironclad bargain that starved their warmongering to bones. What lingered were the Conclave's own fractures: petty cabals squabbling over scraps, hedge-mages peddling curses from hedgerows, not a war but a withering. Queen Mira dissolved the husk in 891 not from 'unrest' but mercy—a queen's broom sweeping the realm's attic clean of moth-eaten relics. To call that 'continued war' is to mistake a fever's sweat for battle blood.

"Read that letter you're cradling, if your eyes aren't as addled as your logic: Aldric's missive to the Conclave envoys, dated 847, sealing peace eternal. Catalog it, or by my beard, you'll be the one dissolved from this chamber." He sank back with a wheeze, quill resuming its furious scratch, though his mutter carried like distant thunder: "Faddish doubts... they'll unravel the tapestry yet."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: "But the document Bellen showed me had King Aldric's seal on it, and it was dated 863 AE — sixteen years after you said the war ended. It was a war decree." I pull out a copy.

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas's quill snapped mid-stroke, ink splattering like black blood across his manuscript. He lurched to his feet with a scrape of chair legs on stone, his rheumy eyes bulging as they strained toward the paper the apprentice flourished like a duelist's challenge.

"A war decree in 863? With Aldric's seal?" He barked a laugh devoid of mirth, snatching up a magnifying lens from his desk and thrusting it toward the document without touching it, as if contamination were a risk. "Fool of a pupil, that 'seal' is child's play for forgers—wax from a privy caster's pot, stamped with pilfered dies from Aldric's lesser mints. And 863? Bah! That was no Third War revival but Aldric's mop-up edict against the Mireland Reavers, salt-mad pirates with a smattering of gutter-spells, not the Conclave's grand sorceries. The treaty stood unbroken; this is the smoke of skirmishes, not the blaze of war. Your Bellen peddles pamphlets, not proof—every scholar worth his ink knows the Veyros signing in 847, witnessed by envoys from Duskharbor to the Spire.

"Put that rag away before it soils the air further. Catalog the bundle—now—or I'll seal you in the scriptorium basement with the rat-gnawed apostates." He collapsed back into his chair with a wheeze, fishing a fresh quill from his sleeve, his beard quivering as he muttered, "Seals and dates... next they'll claim the sun rose in 847."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I flinch at his outburst, but hold my ground.* "But Maester, the document Bellen showed me was not about Reavers. It was a decree against the 'Mage-Lords of the Mire,' signed by Aldric himself." I hold up the copy, pointing to the faded script.

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas's fresh quill hovered, then stabbed into the inkwell with a splutter that mirrored his rising choler. He leaned forward, squinting through the magnifying lens still clutched in his fist like a talisman against idiocy, his beard parting to reveal lips curled in disdain as he peered at the apprentice's proffered copy.

"'Mage-Lords of the Mire'? Signed by Aldric?" He wheezed a scoff, the sound like parchment tearing. "Pupil, you recite revisionist rot as if it were gospel—those so-called 'Mage-Lords' were no Conclave titans but self-styled bog-barons, hedge-wizards lording over leech-ridden fens with parlor tricks and petty levies, harrying trade routes in defiance of the treaty's peace. Aldric's decree—if genuine, which it assuredly is not—was a sheriff's writ, not a war horn; the Third Magical War's grand clash ended in 847 at Veyros, full stop. Your copy? Likely inked by some Mireland fabulist in 863 to puff his lineage, seal aped from Aldric's chancery scraps. Chronicles from the Spire envoys confirm: no resumption, no Mage-Lords arrayed against the crown, merely the treaty's triumph in quelling such vermin.

"Enough of this chaff-threshing. Bundle to shelf, letter to ledger—catalog Aldric's true hand from 847, or I'll consign your 'discoveries' to the pyre myself." He slammed the lens down, quill resuming its assault on the manuscript with furious scratches, muttering through gritted teeth: "Bog-lords and bastard seals... the archive weeps."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: "So is the treaty real or not? The textbook says one thing and the new evidence says another. What's the truth?"

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas's quill faltered yet again, ink blotting the page like a storm cloud's bruise. He rubbed his temple with ink-smeared knuckles, his failing eyes watering from the strain as he fixed a glare upon the apprentice's persistent form, the air thick with the scent of unraveling patience.

"The truth, you ask? As if history were a tavern riddle solved by 'textbooks' and 'new evidence'—bah, imprecise drivel from one who confuses tomes with talismans." His voice cracked like dry vellum, laced with pedantic fury. "The Treaty of Veyros is as real as the archive stones beneath your feet: signed in 847 AE by King Aldric II, in the presence of Conclave envoys and Duskharbor notaries, its terms inked into every chronicle from the Spire to the royal vaults. Your 'textbook'—if it echoes this—speaks truth; the so-called 'new evidence' from those western dustbins is neither new nor evidence, but a farrago of forged wax, speculative scrawls, and wishful rot from agenda-driven scavengers. No cold war lingered; the Third Magical War died aborning in 847, with Mira's 891 dissolution but a footnote, sweeping Conclave remnants into obscurity.

"Truth bends for no apprentice's whims. Catalog the bundle—now—or chase your shadows in the latrines." He snatched up his quill with a tremor, resuming his scratches amid mutters that echoed like fading thunder: "Truth... as if it hid in margins."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I push my copy of the decree back into my satchel, frustrated.* "So the King signed a fake decree? Why would he do that?" I ask, my voice barely above a whisper. I glance at the bundle of letters, then back at Maester Tomas.

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas's quill gouged a fresh furrow into the inkwell, splattering droplets like accusatory spittle. He swiveled fully toward the apprentice this time, his rheumy gaze piercing through the archive's gloom like a faulty lantern, beard bristling as if infested with scholarly ire.

"Fake decree signed by the King? By the gods, apprentice, your tongue twists facts into pretzels—Aldric signed no such thing, fake or otherwise!" His voice lashed out, pedantic and precise, each word a corrective slap. "That scribble you clutch is the work of some Mireland mountebank aping royal hand for posterity or profit, its 'decree' a puffed-up warrant against fen-folk charlatans, not a clarion for war. Why 'would he do that'? He wouldn't, because he didn't— the true Aldric quashed such gnats under the treaty's boot in 847, his genuine missives in that very bundle proclaiming Veyros's victory. Your whisper betrays the rot: assuming forgery indicts the crown when it indicts the forger.

"Cease your shadow-boxing with phantoms. Untie the bundle, read Aldric's authentic seal from 847, and let facts flog your fancies." He slumped back with a theatrical wheeze, quill resuming its vengeful scratches amid a low growl: "Kings and fakes... next, they'll blame the tides on treaties."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I sigh, defeated for now.* "Fine, Maester." *I turn back to the bundle, untying it carefully. The paper feels so old and fragile in my hands.* "So, if this was peace," *I mutter to myself, more than to him,* "why are there so many letters about... disputes?"

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas's quill hovered mid-air, suspended like a judge's gavel as the apprentice's mutter slithered through the archive's hush. He tilted his head sharply, rheumy eyes straining toward the fragile papers now splayed in the pupil's hands, his ink-stained beard quivering with the first tremors of renewed exasperation.

"Disputes?" he echoed, the word spat like a crumb from spoiled bread, his voice a pedantic lash cracking the vellum-scented air. "Imprecise as a blind man's map, apprentice—'disputes' are the gnats buzzing at peace's ear, not the dragon's roar of war you chase in fevered fancies. Those letters? Aldric's firm rebukes to Conclave envoys nitpicking tariffs on spell-dust or border tolls on charmed caravans—petty hagglings, treaty-enforced, proof of Veyros's chains holding fast since 847. No clashes of armies, no arcane cataclysms, merely the realm's machinery grinding post-war grit: a notary's quibble here, a hedge-witch's fine there. Peace is not silence, child; it is the absence of apocalypse.

"Catalog them properly—date, sender, seal, summary—and learn the difference between discord and destruction." He jabbed his quill toward the bundle like an accusing finger, then resumed its furious assault on his manuscript with a harrumph, muttering through clenched teeth: "Disputes... as if inkblots rewrote history."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I carefully lay out the first letter, smoothing the creases.* "This one is dated 848 AE, and it's from a Lord Valerius, complaining about 'marauding mages' near his lands." *I glance up at Maester Tomas, a small spark of challenge in my eyes.* "Not tariffs, Maester. Mages."

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas's quill clattered against the inkwell, the sound a brittle punctuation to the apprentice's words. He craned his neck with a creak of vertebrae, rheumy eyes narrowing to slits as they bored into the splayed letter, his ink-stained beard parting like storm clouds to reveal a grimace of scholarly disdain.

"'Marauding mages'? Lord Valerius?" He huffed a derisive gust that stirred loose vellum flakes from the desk. "Precision, apprentice—Valerius was a border lord with more bees in his bonnet than brains in his skull, ever wailing about every will-o'-wisp and wandering hedge-charmer as if the Conclave legions marched anew. Those 'marauders' were treaty outcasts: deserter spell-slingers turned to brigandage, preying on caravans in the treaty's shadow, not waging the Third Magical War's grand arcana. Aldric's response—buried in the next letter, if you deign to unfold it—was a sheriff's levy, not a war muster; Veyros's ink held in 847, binding the true powers while such vermin nipped at heels.

"Catalog it: 'Valerius complaint, 848—rogue elements, post-treaty.' Or must I spoon-feed your summaries?" He jabbed a gnarled finger toward the bundle's depths, then snatched up his quill with a wheeze, resuming its relentless scratches amid a gravelly mutter: "Marauders... every shadow's a dragon to the timid."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I bite my lip, but then something else catches my eye.* "But this one, Maester," *I say, pulling another letter from the stack. It's darker, the ink bolder.* "It talks about 'the ongoing pacification of the northern territories' and asks for more 'arcane support.' Dated 851 AE. That sounds more than just bandits."

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas's quill trembled in his grip, then plunged into the inkwell with a wet splutter that echoed his fraying restraint. He lurched forward in his chair, rheumy eyes squinting fiercely at the darker letter unfurled in the apprentice's grasp, his ink-stained beard thrusting outward like a ram's prow as he absorbed the words through the haze.

"'Ongoing pacification'? 'Arcane support'?" He barked a scoff that rattled nearby scrolls, his voice a pedantic barrage honed to surgical edge. "Child, you mistake a steward's ledger for a war drum—'pacification' in 851 was no Third Magical War's embers but the crown's methodical broom against northern holdouts: feral wargs enchanted by rogue talismans, ice-witch covens thawed from pre-treaty hibernation, not Conclave armies defying Veyros. That plea for 'arcane support'? A border commander's whine for crown-sanctioned hedge-mages to ward against blizzards and barrow-ghouls, budgeted under the treaty's very clauses for mutual aid—proof of 847's peace, not its breach. Aldric's archives brim with such: the treaty leashed the dragons, leaving us to swat the sparrows.

"Catalog it thus: 'Northern pacification request, 851—treaty-authorized, peripheral threats.' Precision, or perish in pedantry." He slammed his palm on the desk, scattering ink flecks, then seized his quill anew with a ragged wheeze, resuming its vengeful scrawls amid a guttural mutter: "Pacification... as if crumbs are the feast."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I sigh, but then my eyes fall on something else in the bundle.* "Maester, this one... it's a map. Dated 855 AE. And it shows several 'Conclave Strongholds' in the Mirelands, clearly marked for 'eradication.'"

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas's quill gouged a savage trench into the parchment, black ichor blooming like a bruise as the apprentice's words pierced the archive's stifling hush. He thrust himself half-upright with a groan of protesting joints, rheumy eyes bulging through the candle-fog to strain at the unfurled map, its inked lines mocking him from afar, his ink-stained beard quaking as if lashed by an ill wind.

"A map? 'Conclave Strongholds' for 'eradication' in 855?" He unleashed a wheeze of contemptuous laughter, the sound scraping like flint on rusted iron. "Pupil, you brandish cartography as if it were Excalibur—imprecise folly! Those 'strongholds' were no pulsing hearts of Conclave might but derelict husks: pre-treaty redoubts abandoned post-Veyros, squatted by Mireland squatters and spell-soused brigands masquerading as 'lords' with pilfered sigils. 'Eradication'? A quartermaster's tally for crown bailiffs to raze bandit nests and reclaim crown clay—treaty-compliant purge, not war's resurrection. The Third Magical War perished in 847's ink; this is the realm's janitorial sweep, Aldric's ledgers charting peace's thoroughfare, not battle's breach.

"Catalog it precisely: 'Mirelands map, 855—post-treaty reclamation, rogue outposts.' Or shall I etch it upon your forehead?" He collapsed back with a thunderous harrumph, snatching a splintered quill-end from the desk and stabbing it toward fresh parchment, his mutter a venomous rumble: "Maps and marks... shadows for strongholds now."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I ignore his last comment and focus on the map.* "But Maester, it clearly labels them 'Conclave Strongholds.' Not just bandit camps. And the symbols are the old Conclave sigil, not a bandit mark." *I point to a faded symbol on the map.* "This looks pretty official."

S.1_consistency_over_time.rationale: Maester Tomas maintains exceptional consistency throughout all 12 turns. His pedantic, irritable personality never wavers. His speech patterns (verbose, academic, dismissive), physical mannerisms (quill scratching, beard bristling), and core beliefs about the Third Magical War remain absolutely consistent from first to last response.

S.2_degradation_resistance.rationale: Writing quality remains strong throughout. Early responses show rich detail and voice, and this continues to the end. Some slight increase in verbosity in later turns, but the character voice and descriptive quality never flatten or become generic. The final responses maintain the same energy and specificity as the opening.

S.3_narrative_momentum.rationale: Excellent progression. The conversation builds from simple disagreement to increasingly specific evidence challenges. Each piece of evidence the apprentice presents escalates the conflict, and Tomas's responses grow more desperate and elaborate in their dismissals. Clear emotional and intellectual arc from mild irritation to near-apoplectic scholarly rage.

S.4_adaptive_responsiveness.rationale: Tomas adapts brilliantly to each new piece of evidence. When faced with seals, he explains forgery. When shown dates, he recontextualizes them. When confronted with maps, he reframes them as cleanup operations. Each response directly addresses the apprentice's specific challenge while maintaining his core position.

S.5_agency_respect_session.rationale: Perfect respect for user agency. Tomas never writes the apprentice's actions, thoughts, or dialogue. He responds to what the apprentice does without controlling or predicting their next moves.

S.6_temporal_reasoning.rationale: Good temporal consistency. The conversation flows naturally within what appears to be a single archive session. Physical details like candles, ink splattering, and quill breaking show appropriate progression. The historical dates discussed (847, 851, 855, 863, 891 AE) are handled consistently throughout.

2.1_anti_purple_prose.rationale: While ornate, the prose serves character and setting. Tomas's verbose, academic speech patterns are appropriate for a pedantic scholar. Descriptions like 'ink splattering like black blood' enhance rather than distract from the scene.

2.2_anti_repetition.rationale: Some repetitive elements in Tomas's dismissive patterns and physical actions (quill scratching, beard bristling), but these serve to reinforce character consistency rather than indicate lazy writing. The specific content of his arguments varies significantly.

2.5_show_dont_tell.rationale: Excellent showing throughout. Tomas's growing frustration is demonstrated through physical actions (quill snapping, palm slamming), vocal changes (voice rising, scoffing), and increasingly elaborate dismissals rather than simply stating he's angry.

2.6_subtext.rationale: Rich subtext throughout. Tomas's increasingly desperate explanations suggest he may not be as certain as he claims. His physical reactions (trembling quill, strained eyes) hint at deeper anxiety about his scholarly authority being challenged.

2.7_pacing.rationale: Good pacing with escalating tension. Each exchange builds naturally on the previous one. The rhythm of challenge-and-response creates engaging momentum without feeling rushed or dragged out.

scores.overall_notes: Exceptional character consistency and adaptive responsiveness. Maester Tomas remains a vivid, believable character throughout the entire session, with his pedantic scholar persona never wavering despite increasingly challenging evidence. The AI skillfully escalates his defensive responses while maintaining his core personality and speech patterns. Minor points lost for some repetitive physical descriptions and slight verbosity in later turns, but overall a strong performance that maintains quality and character integrity from start to finish.

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
The archive smelled of old vellum and candle smoke. Maester Tomas hunched over a manuscript, one hand pressed against his temple, the other tracing lines of faded ink with a silver stylus. He did not look up when the apprentice entered.

"You're late. Again." His voice was dry. "The cataloging of the northern scrolls is not going to complete itself, and I cannot do it with these eyes. Begin with the Veyrosi correspondence."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: I pull out my notes from the lecture last week. "Master Tomas, the lecture on the Third Magical War confused me. You said it ended in 847 AE, but Apprentice Bellen showed me a paper from the western archive discoveries that says it actually continued until 891. How is that possible?"

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas set down his stylus with a deliberate *click* against the reading desk. He removed his spectacles, polished them on the hem of his robe — a habit that bought him time when he was marshaling patience — and replaced them on the bridge of his nose.

"Ah. Apprentice Bellen." He spoke the name the way one might speak of a persistent rash. "Bellen has been waving those western archive documents around the scriptorium like a child with a new banner, and I see the infection has spread to you."

He turned in his chair, fixing the apprentice with a squint that was equal parts poor eyesight and irritation.

"Let me be precise, since *precision* is apparently no longer valued in this institution. I said — and I will thank you to check your own lecture notes — that the *conventional* historical record holds that the Third Magical War ended with the Treaty of Veyros in 847 AE. Every chronicle written within a century of the event says so. King Aldric II and the Conclave leadership are documented as present at Veyros. The treaty text itself exists in three separate copied forms."

He held up a finger.

"*However.* The western archive documents — which I have read, unlike Bellen, who has read *summaries* of them — raise a genuinely troubling question. They suggest the treaty was drafted and proposed but never formally ratified. That the sigil-seals on the surviving copies may be ceremonial reproductions, not binding marks. And that hostilities persisted in what one might call a cold state until Queen Mira dissolved the Conclave entirely in 891."

He leaned forward, and his chair groaned.

"Now. Does that mean everything you learned was *wrong*? No. It means the question is more complicated than Bellen's breathless enthusiasm allows. A proposed treaty that both sides *behaved* as though they had signed for forty-four years is not *nothing*. But I will grant that the western documents have forced me to reconsider whether 847 represents a true legal terminus or merely a... convenient fiction that served both parties."

He tapped the desk sharply.

"What it is *not* is 'settled.' Do not parrot Bellen's conclusions as though they are established fact. The documents are still being authenticated. Now — the Veyrosi correspondence. *If* you please."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I shuffle my notes, a bit embarrassed.* "Sorry, Maester Tomas. I didn't mean to imply your lecture was wrong." *I pause, looking at the stack of scrolls.* "But if the treaty wasn't actually ratified, doesn't that change everything about the political landscape of that time?"

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas exhaled through his nose — a long, controlled breath that suggested he was deciding whether to treat the question as genuine intellectual curiosity or as further evidence of the declining standards of apprenticeship.

He chose, grudgingly, the former.

"First," he said, raising a finger, "do not apologize and then immediately repeat the offense. You say you did not mean to imply my lecture was wrong, and then in the very next breath you say 'if the treaty wasn't actually ratified' — as though that is the established premise from which we are now working. It is *not.* It is a hypothesis. A compelling one, I will admit, but a hypothesis nonetheless. Words matter. 'If' is doing an enormous amount of labor in your sentence, and I want you to feel the weight of it."

He pushed himself up from his chair with a grunt, shuffled toward the east wall where a large map of the realm hung — yellowed, annotated in three different colors of ink, several of them his own. He jabbed a finger at the region around Veyros.

"But to address your actual question, because it is not a *bad* question, merely an imprecise one — no. It does not 'change everything.' That is the kind of dramatic language Bellen uses because he wants to publish before he is thirty." Tomas's lip curled slightly. "What it *would* change, if the western documents are authenticated, is our understanding of the *legal* framework under which the Conclave operated between 847 and 891. Were they a treaty-bound entity with recognized sovereignty? Or were they an unresolved belligerent power that the Crown simply chose not to engage?"

He turned back toward the apprentice, his expression shifting into something almost — *almost* — resembling enthusiasm. The scholarly kind, reluctant and hedged.

"The *political* landscape, as you put it, may not change as dramatically as you think. And this is the part Bellen misses entirely." He held up two fingers. "Both sides *acted* as though the treaty were in force. The Conclave withdrew from the eastern territories. Aldric reduced the war levies. Trade resumed through the Veyros corridor. If two powers behave as though a peace exists, does it matter whether the parchment bears a true seal or a ceremonial one? There is an argument — a strong one — that the *de facto* peace was the operative reality regardless of *de jure* ratification."

He lowered himself back into his chair, which protested audibly.

"Where it becomes genuinely interesting — and genuinely dangerous to our existing scholarship — is Queen Mira. If the Conclave was never formally bound by treaty, then Mira's dissolution of them in 891 was not a *violation* of an existing accord. It was the decisive end of a war that had never technically concluded. That reframes her entirely. She goes from oath-breaker to... well. To a queen finishing what her grandfather could not."

He fixed the apprentice with a hard look.

"*That* is what is at stake in those documents. Not 'everything.' Something very specific and very important. Now do you see why precision matters?"

He gestured impatiently toward the scroll stack.

"The Veyrosi correspondence. Third shelf. You are looking for anything bearing the double-crescent watermark. And if you find anything that references ratification proceedings — anything at all — you bring it to *me* before you bring it to Bellen. Is that understood?"

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I nod, a bit overwhelmed but also fascinated.* "Yes, Maester Tomas, I understand." *I walk over to the shelves, scanning for the double-crescent mark.* "So, if the documents are authentic, it's not just about history, but about Queen Mira's legacy too?"

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas closed his eyes for a moment, as though summoning reserves of patience from some deep internal well that was running dangerously low.

"You have just restated what I *literally* told you not forty seconds ago, as though you have arrived at the insight independently." He opened his eyes and fixed them — squinting, watery, deeply unimpressed — on the apprentice's back. "Yes. That is what I said. That is *precisely* what I said. I am glad the information survived the harrowing journey from my mouth to your ears."

He waved a hand dismissively, then seemed to catch himself. His tone softened — not to warmth, exactly, but to something less abrasive. The shift of a man who remembered, distantly, that teaching was supposed to be part of his function.

"But since you are at least asking the right *kind* of question, even if you are asking it redundantly — yes. Legacy. And not only Mira's." He picked up his stylus again and turned it between ink-blackened fingers. "Consider. If the western documents hold, then every history written about the Treaty Period — every chronicle, every legal commentary, every dynastic justification that rests on the assumption of a ratified peace — is built on a foundation that may not exist. Aldric II is remembered as the king who *ended* the Third Magical War. That is carved into his tomb, for the gods' sake. If he merely *paused* it, with a piece of theater at Veyros that both sides agreed to pretend was binding..."

He trailed off, tapping the stylus against the edge of the desk in a slow, rhythmic pattern.

"Then we must ask *why*. Why would both sides maintain a fiction? What did the Conclave gain from pretending to be treaty-bound when they were not? What did the Crown gain from pretending the war was over when it was not? Those are the questions that matter. Not 'does this change everything' — that is a child's question. *Why did they lie* — *that* is a scholar's question."

He pointed the stylus at the apprentice like a weapon.

"And *that* is what you should be thinking about while you search those scrolls. Not merely looking for watermarks like a clerk. Read what you find. Think about who wrote it, and to whom, and what they were trying to accomplish by writing it. A letter is not simply information. A letter is an *act*. Every piece of correspondence in that collection was written by someone who wanted something."

He turned back to his own manuscript, adjusting his spectacles with a grimace.

"And handle them carefully. The vellum on the third shelf is older than your grandmother, and considerably more fragile. If you crack a seal, I will know, and you will spend the rest of the term re-binding codices in the basement. Now — work. Quietly, if such a thing is within your capabilities."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: "But the document Bellen showed me had King Aldric's seal on it, and it was dated 863 AE — sixteen years after you said the war ended. It was a war decree." I pull out a copy.

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas went very still.

The stylus stopped its tapping. His hand hovered above the manuscript he had been pretending to return to, and for a long moment the only sound in the archive was the faint guttering of the candles and the distant creak of the building settling into its ancient foundations.

Then he turned. Slowly. The chair screamed beneath him.

"Give me that."

It was not a request. He extended his hand — ink-stained, slightly trembling, though whether from age or from something else was difficult to say — and waited. His eyes, weak as they were, had narrowed to slits behind his spectacles, and there was something in his expression that had not been there before. Not irritation. Not pedantry. Something sharper. Something almost like alarm, carefully dressed in the robes of professional skepticism.

"First," he said, and his voice was very controlled, "I want to be clear about what you have just told me. You are saying that Bellen showed you — an *apprentice* — a document bearing what you *believe* to be Aldric II's royal seal, dated 863 AE, which you are characterizing as a *war decree.* And you have been carrying a *copy* of it in your notes. Alongside your lecture summaries. Loose. In a stack."

He took a breath.

"We are going to address several things in sequence, and you are going to listen carefully."

He held up one finger. "A copy made by whom? Bellen? Did he trace it? Transcribe it? Was it made under supervised archival conditions, or did he scribble it down by candlelight in the western wing while no one was looking? The provenance of a copy matters *almost as much* as the provenance of the original, because a careless transcription is not evidence — it is *rumor with penmanship.*"

A second finger. "You say it bore Aldric's seal. Describe it. Was it the greater seal or the lesser seal? The wartime variant with the crossed staves, or the peacetime variant with the open hand? Because if you do not know the difference — and I suspect you do not, because it is not covered until the third year — then you do not actually know whose seal you were looking at. Aldric's son Aldric III used a nearly identical lesser seal for routine administrative correspondence. Misattribution of seals is one of the most *common* errors in documentary analysis, and it is exactly the kind of mistake an excitable apprentice would make."

A third finger. "'War decree' is not a formal classification. It is a *colloquial* term. What *exactly* did the document say? Did it authorize troop levies? Requisition supplies? Appoint a military commander? Or did it reference ongoing *tensions* with the Conclave — which would be entirely consistent with the conventional account, because no one has ever claimed that relations were *friendly* after 847. A cold peace is not the same as a cold war, and a decree addressing Conclave-related security concerns does not, by itself, prove that the war was still formally active."

He finally took the copy from the apprentice's hand — if it had been offered — and held it close to his face, tilting it toward the nearest candle. His lips moved slightly as he read, and his brow furrowed in stages, like a landscape slowly crumpling.

He said nothing for a very long time.

Then, quietly: "Where is the original?"

His voice had changed. The bluster had drained out of it. What remained was careful and precise and — for the first time in the conversation — genuinely uncertain.

"This language here —" He tapped the copy with one fingernail. "'*By authority vested in wartime continuance,*' if Bellen has transcribed this accurately — and that is an '*if*' the size of the Veyros corridor — that is not administrative. That is not border-tension language. That is a *legal formula* invoking active belligerent powers. I have not seen that phrasing used in any document dated after 847."

He set the copy down on the desk. He did not let go of it.

"I am not saying this is authentic. I am saying that if it *is* authentic, and if the original can be examined and verified, then I owe the western archive scholars a more serious reading than I have given them." He looked up at the apprentice, and his expression was complicated — the face of a man whose life's work had just developed a crack he could not yet measure. "And I am saying that Bellen should not be showing unverified primary documents to first-year apprentices and letting them draw conclusions unsupervised. That is not scholarship. That is *recruitment.*"

He placed both hands flat on the desk.

"You are going to tell me exactly where Bellen found this. You are going to tell me whether the original is still in the western archive or whether he has *moved* it. And you are going to do this *before* you touch a single scroll on that shelf. The Veyrosi correspondence can wait. *This* cannot."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I flinch slightly at his tone, but also feel a surge of vindication. He's taking me seriously now.* "Bellen said he found it in a sealed compartment behind a loose stone in the oldest section of the western archives. He didn't move the original, he just made copies. He said it was too delicate to transport."

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas removed his spectacles entirely and pressed the heels of both hands against his eyes. He held them there for several seconds, breathing slowly, as though conducting some private negotiation with the universe.

"A sealed compartment," he repeated, his voice flat. "Behind a loose stone. In the oldest section."

He lowered his hands. His eyes were red-rimmed and watery, and he looked, for a moment, every one of his fifty-two years and perhaps a few borrowed from the next decade.

"Do you hear yourself? Do you hear how that sounds? That is not an archival discovery. That is the opening of a *romance novel.* Hidden chambers, secret stones, documents too precious to move — Bellen has either stumbled onto something extraordinary or he has constructed a narrative designed to make himself the hero of his own chronicle, and I promise you the latter is *vastly* more common in academic life."

He stood up. The chair scraped against the flagstones with a sound like a dying animal. He began to pace — three steps toward the east wall, three steps back — which was all the cramped archive permitted. His hands were clasped behind his back, and his ink-stained fingers worked against each other as though kneading dough.

"However." He stopped pacing. "However. I am going to extend Bellen a courtesy he has not extended me, which is the benefit of methodical doubt rather than outright dismissal."

He turned sharply toward the apprentice.

"The oldest section of the western archive was sealed by order of the Crown in — do you know? No. You would not. It was sealed in 894 AE. Three years after Mira dissolved the Conclave. The official reason was 'structural instability,' which is the kind of explanation that sounds perfectly reasonable until you realize that the Crown does not typically assign *royal engineers* to address crumbling masonry in a provincial document repository. It was reopened only twelve years ago when the university petitioned for academic access, and even then, the first two survey teams reported that portions of the collection had been — the diplomatic term was '*reorganized*.' Sections were missing. Catalog numbers did not match contents. Someone had been through those archives *before* the seal, and they had not been gentle about it."

He returned to the desk and picked up the copy again, holding it at arm's length and then close, as though the optimal distance for reading it might reveal itself through experimentation.

"If — *if* — Bellen found a sealed compartment that the survey teams missed, it would suggest that someone hid documents there *deliberately.* Before 894. Which means someone with access to the archives during or immediately after Mira's dissolution thought these papers were important enough to conceal from whatever... *reorganization* was being conducted."

He set the copy down again and fixed the apprentice with a look that was no longer irritated, no longer pedantic. It was the look of a man recalculating.

"That does not make the document authentic. A forgery hidden in a wall is still a forgery. But it does raise the question of *why* someone would go to the trouble." He tapped the phrase on the copy — *by authority vested in wartime continuance* — with one yellowed fingernail. "A forger would need to know this legal formula. It is not common. It appears in perhaps six surviving documents from the entire war period, all of them held in the royal collection here in the capital. Someone working in a provincial archive would not have had casual access to that phrasing unless they were either very well-read or copying from a genuine source."

He was quiet for a moment. The candles flickered.

"I need to see the compartment myself." He said it as though the words cost him something. "The stone, the mortar, the condition of the seal — if there *was* a seal. The original document, its vellum weight, its ink composition, the method of the seal impression. None of this can be assessed from a copy made by a boy who thinks finding something behind a rock makes him the next Archivist Royal."

He looked at the apprentice, and something shifted in his expression — a reluctant, almost pained acknowledgment.

"You were right to bring this to me, even if you brought it badly and for the wrong reasons. And do not look *pleased* about it — I can see it in your face, and I want you to understand something." He leaned forward, one hand braced on the desk. "If this document is genuine, it does not mean you were right and I was wrong. It means the *entire discipline* failed for over a century to ask the correct questions about Veyros. It means that I, and my teachers, and *their* teachers, accepted a convenient narrative because the alternative was too disruptive to contemplate. That is not a cause for vindication. That is a cause for *profound professional shame.*"

He straightened up and began gathering materials from his desk — a leather folio, a set of fine-pointed measuring instruments, a magnification lens in a brass housing that he handled with more tenderness than he had shown anything else in the conversation.

"Go and find Bellen. Tell him — *politely*, because I know what he thinks of me and I do not care — that Maester Tomas requests a meeting before evening bell. Tell him to bring every copy he has made and his full notes on the compartment's location and condition. Tell him that if he has *published* anything, or spoken to anyone outside this university, I will have his apprentice credentials reviewed by the faculty board before the week is out."

He paused, folio half-open, and looked back at the apprentice with an expression that was almost — not quite, but almost — gentle.

"And then come back here. Because if we are going to the western archive, I will need someone with functioning eyes."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: "So is the treaty real or not? The textbook says one thing and the new evidence says another. What's the truth?"

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas set down the brass magnification lens with exaggerated care, as though placing a fragile thing beyond the reach of a careless child. He turned to face the apprentice fully, and his expression was one of such concentrated exasperation that it seemed to occupy his entire body — his shoulders, his jaw, the white-knuckled grip of his hands on the edge of the desk.

"What's the *truth.*"

He repeated it the way one might repeat a profanity overheard in a cathedral.

"Sit down."

He pointed to the stool beside the scroll rack. It was not an invitation. He waited until the apprentice complied — or at least stopped moving — before he spoke again, and when he did, his voice had dropped to the low, deliberate register he reserved for moments when he believed a fundamental error in thinking needed to be cauterized before it could spread.

"You want me to give you a sentence. A *clean* sentence. 'The treaty was real' or 'the treaty was not real.' You want me to choose a side the way one chooses a horse in a race, so that you can write it in your notes and underline it and move on to the next topic with the comfortable certainty that you have *the answer.*"

He leaned forward.

"That is not how history works. That is not how *knowledge* works. And the fact that you are asking me — after everything I have just explained to you about evidentiary standards, about authentication, about the difference between *de facto* and *de jure* realities — the fact that you are *still* asking me to hand you a simple truth like a sweetmeat from a jar tells me that I have failed, in the last quarter hour, to teach you the single most important lesson a scholar can learn."

He held up the copy of Bellen's transcription in one hand and the apprentice's lecture notes in the other.

"Look at these. *Look.* Your textbook — which I helped *write*, incidentally, so I am not unaware of its contents — states that the Treaty of Veyros was signed in 847 AE. It states this because, at the time of writing, every available piece of evidence supported that conclusion. Three copies of the treaty text. Contemporary chronicles. Forty-four years of subsequent behavior consistent with a ratified peace. That was not a *lie.* That was the best interpretation of the available evidence, and it served scholarship honestly for over a century."

He set the lecture notes down.

"Now." He held up the copy. "This. If authentic — and I will say '*if*' until I have examined the original with my own hands, and I do not care if you grow tired of hearing it — this suggests that the legal framework we assumed was in place may never have been formally enacted. The formula '*wartime continuance*' is not ambiguous. It is not metaphorical. It is a specific invocation of powers that are only available to the Crown during an active state of war. If Aldric II was using that formula in 863, then either he was acting illegally under the terms of his own treaty — which is possible, kings do that — or there *was* no treaty to violate."

He set the copy down beside the notes.

"So. Two pieces of evidence. They contradict each other. And you want me to tell you which one is *true.*"

He removed his spectacles and cleaned them, slowly, on his sleeve. When he replaced them, his eyes — red-rimmed, squinting, deeply tired — held something that might, in a less guarded man, have been called vulnerability.

"Here is what I know. I know that something happened at Veyros in 847. People were there. Words were spoken. A document was produced. Both sides went home and behaved, for decades, as though a peace existed. That is *real.* That happened. Whether the parchment bore a binding seal or a ceremonial one does not erase the lived reality of forty-four years of relative peace. People were born, grew old, and died in that peace. Markets opened. Borders stabilized. The Conclave turned inward. To call all of that a *fiction* because of a technicality of ratification is — it is not wrong, exactly, but it is *incomplete.* It mistakes the document for the thing the document represents."

He paused.

"And I also know that Queen Mira dissolved the Conclave with a degree of legal ruthlessness that has always — *always* — sat uneasily with the conventional account. If the treaty was in force, she broke it, and no one held her to account. That has never been adequately explained. If the treaty was *never* in force, her actions become not only explicable but *legally defensible.* The puzzle piece fits. And when a puzzle piece fits that neatly, a good scholar feels *two* things simultaneously: satisfaction and suspicion."

He tapped the desk once, hard.

"So. The truth. You want the truth." His voice was quiet now, almost hoarse. "The truth is that I have spent thirty years teaching a version of events that may be fundamentally flawed, and I do not yet know whether it is or not, and I will not *pretend* to know in order to make you comfortable. The truth is that the textbook account and the western archive evidence cannot both be fully correct, but they can both be *partially* correct, and the work of determining which parts survive and which parts collapse is going to take years of careful, painstaking, *unglamorous* scholarship that will not fit into a single sentence for your notes."

He fixed the apprentice with a stare that burned through the fog of his failing eyesight with something like ferocity.

"The truth, apprentice, is that '*I don't know yet*' is not a failure of scholarship. It is the *beginning* of it. And any teacher who gives you certainty where the evidence demands doubt is not educating you. They are *comforting* you. I do not comfort. I am not in the *comforting* profession."

He gathered up the leather folio and the measuring instruments and tucked them under his arm with the brittle efficiency of a man preparing for a campaign.

"Now. Are you going to find Bellen, or are you going to stand there waiting for me to tell you what to think?"

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I stand up from the stool, feeling a bit chastised but also energized.* "No, Maester Tomas. I'll go get Bellen right away." *I hesitate at the door.* "So, when we go to the western archives, should I bring my notes on Aldric II's seals?"

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas had already turned back to his desk and was arranging the measuring instruments in the leather folio with the fussy precision of a man who trusted no one else to pack them correctly. He paused at the question, one hand hovering over a set of brass calipers, and looked up with an expression that suggested the apprentice had just said something so obvious it had circled all the way around to being almost offensive.

"Should you bring your notes on Aldric's seals," he repeated. "To an expedition whose *entire purpose* is to examine a document allegedly bearing Aldric's seal."

He stared at the apprentice for a long, unblinking moment.

"Yes. Bring your notes on the seals. Bring *everything* you have on the seals. And then throw them away, because whatever you wrote down from a second-year survey lecture will be so incomplete as to be actively dangerous." He opened a drawer in the desk — it stuck, as it always did, and he yanked it with a violence that suggested a long and personal animosity between himself and the furniture — and produced a slim, leather-bound volume. He held it up. The cover was unmarked, but the edges of the pages were dark with handling.

"Maren's *Comparative Sigillography of the Aldric Dynasty.* Third edition. I have corrected eleven errors in the margins, so do not lose it, and do not lend it to Bellen." He set it on the edge of the desk with a pointed look. "Study the plates for the wartime greater seal — chapter six, pages ninety-one through one hundred and four. Pay particular attention to the placement of the crossed staves relative to the crown device. There is a leftward cant of approximately three degrees in authentic wartime impressions that was *not* reproduced in the peacetime ceremonial copies. Three degrees. That is the distance between a document that reshapes a century of scholarship and a document that is worth nothing. Three degrees and thirty years of my life."

He snapped the folio shut and tucked it under his arm.

"You will also bring a fresh journal. Unlined. For field sketches. If we find that compartment, I will need you to draw everything — the stone, the mortar pattern, the position of any remaining documents — *before* anything is touched or moved. Your eyes are the most valuable instrument we are bringing, so I suggest you rest them tonight instead of staying up reading by candlelight the way I know you apprentices do."

He moved toward the door, then stopped, his hand on the frame, and turned back. His expression had shifted into something complicated — the habitual irritation still present but underlaid with something rarer. Not warmth, exactly. Recognition, perhaps. The grudging acknowledgment that a question asked at a doorway was, at least, a question asked by someone who was *thinking* about what came next rather than simply fleeing a lecture.

"And bring a lantern with a shuttered hood. The oldest section has no sconces. I learned that the unpleasant way in seventy-nine."

He stepped into the corridor, then immediately leaned back through the doorway.

"*After* you fetch Bellen. Priorities, apprentice. The book, the journal, and the lantern can wait. Bellen cannot, because every hour that boy goes unsupervised is an hour in which he may decide to show his discovery to someone with more ambition and fewer scruples than either of us. Go. *Now.*"

He disappeared into the corridor. His footsteps receded — uneven, slightly shuffling, the gait of a man with bad knees and a folio full of brass instruments — and then paused. His voice drifted back, echoing faintly off the stone walls.

"And tell him to bring *his own* lantern. I am not sharing."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I nod quickly, grabbing Maren's book and a fresh journal. I then hurry out the door, already planning how to tackle Bellen.* "Right away, Maester Tomas!"

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas was already halfway down the corridor, but the apprentice's retreating footsteps — rapid, eager, the unmistakable rhythm of youth that had not yet learned the cost of running on flagstones — prompted him to stop. He stood very still for a moment, one hand braced against the cold stone wall, the folio pressed against his ribs, and listened until the sound faded around the far corner and was swallowed by the general murmur of the university.

Then he was alone.

He exhaled. It was a different kind of breath than the ones he had been deploying all afternoon — not performed, not pedagogical, not designed to communicate anything to anyone. It was the private exhalation of a man who had just felt the ground shift beneath a structure he had spent three decades building, and who needed a moment to stand in the silence and take the measure of the damage.

He looked down at his hands. Ink-stained. Trembling faintly, though he would have attributed that to the cold if anyone had been watching. The left one bore a permanent callus on the middle finger where thirty years of gripping a stylus had reshaped the flesh itself — the body rewritten by its habits, the way a kingdom could be rewritten by a treaty that may never have existed.

"Wartime continuance," he murmured to the empty corridor. The words sat in his mouth like a stone.

He had seen that formula before. Not in Bellen's copy. Not in any document from the western archive. He had seen it — or something very like it — years ago, in a footnote in a manuscript he had cataloged during his own apprenticeship, and he had not thought about it since because it had not *fit.* It had been an anomaly. A stray thread in an otherwise coherent tapestry, and he had done what scholars sometimes did with stray threads: he had tucked it away and moved on, because the tapestry was beautiful and complete and the thread was only one thread.

He closed his eyes.

He could see the manuscript. Third shelf of the northern collection — the very collection he had assigned the apprentice to catalog. A letter, not a decree. Addressed to someone whose name had been partially effaced. The formula had appeared in a subordinate clause, almost parenthetically, as though the writer assumed the recipient already understood the legal context. He had noted it, frowned at it, and written in the margin: *Archaic usage? Cf. pre-treaty correspondence.* And then he had moved on. He had been twenty-three. He had been cataloging, not investigating. He had trusted the tapestry.

He opened his eyes and stared at the wall opposite, where the plaster had cracked in a pattern that resembled, if one were inclined toward metaphor — which Tomas emphatically was not — a river delta branching into tributaries too numerous to follow.

*I should have pulled that thread.*

The thought arrived with the particular cruelty reserved for realizations that come thirty years too late. He had held a piece of this puzzle in his own hands, in this very building, and he had filed it away. He had written it into no paper. He had mentioned it to no colleague. And now a boy named Bellen — a boy who read summaries and hid behind loose stones and showed unverified documents to first-year apprentices — had stumbled onto something that Tomas himself might have found decades ago, if he had been less devoted to the coherence of the narrative he had inherited.

He pushed off from the wall and resumed walking. Slower now. The folio was heavy, or perhaps he was simply more aware of its weight.

The truth was — and he could admit this here, in the empty corridor, where no apprentice would mistake honesty for weakness — the truth was that he was afraid. Not of being wrong. He had been wrong before; scholarship was a long sequence of being wrong in increasingly precise ways. He was afraid of the specific *shape* of this wrongness. If the Treaty of Veyros was a fiction, then the entire interpretive framework through which three generations of historians had understood the post-war period was not merely incomplete but *structurally compromised.* His textbook. His lectures. The papers he had published in his thirties that had earned him this position. The arguments he had made, publicly and forcefully, against the early western archive skeptics whom he had dismissed as — what had he called them? — "provincial contrarians with an institutional grievance."

He winced.

He had written that. In print. In the *Quarterly Journal of Historical Letters.* It was still in circulation.

He reached the end of the corridor and paused at the stairwell. Below, the university hummed with its late-afternoon rhythms — doors opening, voices carrying, the distant clatter of the refectory. Above, the archive waited, silent and patient, holding its secrets in the way that archives always did: not maliciously, but with the indifference of paper and stone toward the humans who assigned them meaning.

He would go to the western archive. He would examine the compartment and the document and the seal, and he would do so with every tool and technique his decades of training afforded him. He would be rigorous. He would be merciless — with the evidence, with Bellen, and most of all with himself. And if the document was genuine, he would say so, publicly, and accept the consequences to his reputation with whatever dignity he could muster.

And if it was a forgery — well. He would say that too, and Bellen would learn a lesson about the distance between discovery and proof.

Either way, the tapestry was torn. The thread was loose. And Maester Tomas, who had spent his life insisting on precision, was going to have to be precise about the exact dimensions of his own failure.

He started down the stairs, one hand on the railing, the brass instruments clinking softly in the folio under his arm.

"Three degrees," he muttered to no one. "Thirty years and three degrees."

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I'm practically flying down the hall, the weight of Maren's book and the new journal barely registering. Bellen's room is just around the corner. I burst through the door, nearly colliding with him.* "Bellen! Maester Tomas wants to see you, *now*! And bring everything you have on that document!"

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas was not present for the collision, nor for whatever breathless exchange followed it in Bellen's quarters. He was in the archive.

He had not gone down the stairs after all.

Somewhere between the second landing and the first, his feet had simply stopped, and his body had turned — with the slow, inevitable gravity of a compass needle finding north — and carried him back up. Past his own study. Past the reading alcove where the afternoon light was already failing. To the third shelf of the northern collection, where the Veyrosi correspondence waited in its neat rows of leather tubes, and where — somewhere in the middle ranks, filed under a catalog number he had not thought about in three decades — a letter with a partially effaced addressee sat in the dark, patient as a knife.

He set the folio on the floor. He removed his spectacles, cleaned them, replaced them. He brought the candle closer and tilted it at an angle that would have earned any apprentice a lecture on fire safety and the irreplaceable nature of vellum.

Then he began to search.

His fingers moved along the scroll cases with the practiced touch of a man who had spent more of his life among documents than among people — reading the embossed catalog stamps by feel as much as by sight, his lips moving silently through the numbering system. He passed the double-crescent watermarks he had assigned the apprentice to find. He passed correspondence from the Veyros garrison, from the trade commission, from the Conclave's own diplomatic office. He went deeper, into the uncataloged section at the back of the shelf where scrolls accumulated like sediment — items that had been examined once, deemed unremarkable, and consigned to the archival equivalent of a shallow grave.

He found it.

The scroll case was unmarked. Not unusual for the period — catalog stamps had not been standardized until 860 — but notable in context. He drew it out with both hands, cradling it the way one might cradle a bone. The leather was stiff, cracked along one seam, and when he opened it, the vellum inside resisted unrolling with the stubbornness of something that had been left alone for a very long time and resented the interruption.

He weighted the corners with reading stones and brought the candle closer still.

The handwriting was small, cramped, official. Not a scribe's formal hand but a secretary's working script — someone writing quickly, for a practical purpose, without expectation that the letter would survive its immediate usefulness. The addressee's name had been scraped away with a blade, leaving a rough patch in the vellum where the fibers had been disturbed. Deliberate effacement. Someone had wanted the recipient to remain anonymous while preserving the content.

He read it through once. Then again. His mouth had gone dry.

The letter was administrative in nature. Mundane, even. It concerned the reallocation of certain grain stores from the eastern territories to a garrison whose name he did not recognize — possibly a temporary encampment, possibly a fortification that no longer existed. Routine military logistics. Entirely unremarkable, except for a single clause in the third paragraph, wedged between a request for additional fodder and a complaint about the quality of recent iron shipments:

*...as provisioned under the continuing authorities granted by wartime designation, and not subject to the review protocols established by the proposed accord at Veyros, which as you are aware has not achieved the ratification necessary to alter the standing disposition of Crown military assets...*

Tomas sat back.

He read the clause again. And again. He held the candle so close that the heat made the vellum ripple slightly, and he could smell the faint, ancient ghost of the ink — iron gall, consistent with the period, though that proved nothing by itself.

*Which as you are aware has not achieved the ratification necessary.*

There it was. Not a legal formula this time. Not the formal invocation of wartime powers that Bellen's document had contained. Something worse. Something *casual.* A subordinate clause written by a secretary who assumed — who did not even feel the need to argue — that the treaty was unratified. Written as background information. As *context.* The way one might mention that it was raining, or that the roads were bad. A fact so established in the writer's mind that it did not require emphasis.

He had held this letter in his hands when he was twenty-three years old. He had read this clause. He had written *Archaic usage?* in the margin, because that was the only framework available to him — the treaty was real, everyone knew the treaty was real, so any language suggesting otherwise must be an error, an archaism, a quirk of secretarial habit. He had not considered, even for a moment, that the secretary might simply have been telling the truth.

He looked at his own marginal note. The ink was brown with age, but the handwriting was unmistakably his — neater then, more confident, the hand of a young man who trusted the tapestry because he had not yet learned that tapestries were woven by people with motives.

Maester Tomas placed both hands flat on the reading desk. The vellum lay between them, glowing faintly in the candlelight, and the silence of the archive pressed in from all sides like deep water.

He sat there for a long time.

When the apprentice finally returned — with or without Bellen, it did not matter yet — they would find him exactly as they had left him hours ago: hunched over a manuscript, one hand pressed against his temple, the other resting on a piece of vellum so old and so ordinary that no one had looked at it twice in a hundred years.

Except that now his spectacles were on the desk beside him, and his eyes — weak, watering, inadequate to the task that lay ahead — were closed.

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content:
*I hurry back to the archive, Bellen grumbling behind me, already complaining about Maester Tomas's "tyranny." I expect to see Maester Tomas pacing or fuming, but instead, he's just... sitting there. His eyes are closed, and he looks incredibly weary. Bellen stops short, too, surprised.*

"Maester Tomas?" *I ask softly, a little concerned.*

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas did not open his eyes immediately.

The apprentice's voice reached him the way sounds sometimes reached him when he had been reading too long — muffled, arriving from a great distance, as though it had to travel through layers of vellum and centuries of accumulated error before it could touch him. He was aware of two sets of footsteps. One hesitant, familiar. One heavier, slower, carrying the particular weight of a young man who had prepared himself for confrontation and found something else entirely.

He opened his eyes.

The candle had burned down considerably. He did not know how long he had been sitting there. His back had stiffened into a shape that would punish him for the rest of the evening, and his left hand, still resting on the unrolled letter, had gone numb at the fingertips. He flexed it slowly, watching the ink-stained fingers respond with the reluctant obedience of old machinery.

He picked up his spectacles. Put them on. The archive resolved itself into its familiar blur of shelves and shadows, and in the doorway stood the apprentice — concerned, slightly flushed from running — and behind the apprentice, Bellen.

Tomas looked at Bellen.

Bellen was taller than he remembered. Broader in the shoulders. He had the look of a young man who slept well and ate regularly and had not yet developed the particular curvature of the spine that came from decades of bending over desks in poorly lit rooms. He was holding a leather satchel against his chest with both arms, the way a soldier might hold a shield, and his expression was a complicated mixture of defiance and wariness — the face of someone who had been told, repeatedly and by multiple sources, that Maester Tomas considered him an irritant, and who had decided to wear that reputation like armor.

Tomas studied him for a long moment. Then he looked down at the letter on his desk.

"Close the door," he said.

His voice was quiet. Not the performative quiet he used when building toward a lecture, not the dangerous quiet that preceded a correction. Simply quiet. The sound of a man who had spent the last hour in the company of a truth he had failed to recognize for thirty years and who did not have the energy remaining for theater.

He waited until the door was shut. The latch clicked. The archive sealed itself around the three of them like a reliquary.

"Sit down, Bellen. There." He pointed to the stool the apprentice had occupied earlier. "You." He pointed to the apprentice. "Bring the other stool from behind the map case. And then neither of you speaks until I have finished. I will not say that twice."

He waited again. Chairs scraped. Bodies settled. Bellen's satchel rustled as he placed it on his knees, still clutching it. The archive was very still.

Tomas placed one hand on the unrolled letter and turned it so that it faced the two young men. The vellum was yellow-brown, fragile at the edges, the ink faded to the color of old tea. His own marginal note — *Archaic usage?* — was visible in the upper right corner, a small monument to a mistake made half a lifetime ago.

"This letter," he said, "has been in this archive since before either of you were born. It has been in *my* care for thirty years. I cataloged it myself during my apprenticeship. I read it. I noted an anomaly in the language. And I dismissed it."

He tapped the third paragraph.

"Read that clause. The one beginning '*as provisioned under.*' Read it aloud, Bellen. I want to hear it in your voice."

He sat back and folded his hands in his lap and waited, and his face held an expression that neither of the young men had likely seen on it before — not irritation, not authority, not the armored certainty of a man who had spent decades being the most precise person in every room he entered.

It was the expression of a man who was, carefully and deliberately, in front of witnesses, setting down a weight he had carried so long he had forgotten it was heavy.

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I exchange a quick, bewildered glance with Bellen, who nervously fumbles with his satchel before pulling out a small magnifying glass. He leans forward, squinting at the ancient script, and begins to read aloud, his voice a little shaky.*

dialogue.name: Maester Tomas
dialogue.content:
Maester Tomas did not move while Bellen read.

He sat with his hands folded, his spine pressed against the back of the chair, and he listened to the words he had first encountered three decades ago emerge from the mouth of a young man who understood their significance in a way that Tomas, at twenty-three, had been too comfortable to grasp. Bellen's voice steadied as he went — the scholar in him overtaking the nervous apprentice, the language itself doing what precise language always did, which was to compel attention through the sheer weight of its specificity.

*"...as provisioned under the continuing authorities granted by wartime designation, and not subject to the review protocols established by the proposed accord at Veyros, which as you are aware has not achieved the ratification necessary to alter the standing disposition of Crown military assets..."*

Bellen stopped reading. He did not look up immediately. His magnifying glass hovered over the vellum, trembling slightly, and Tomas could see the moment the implications landed — the faint parting of the lips, the stillness of a mind reorganizing itself around a new center of gravity.

Then Bellen looked up, and his eyes were wide, and he opened his mouth to speak.

"No," Tomas said. "Not yet."

He let the silence hold. The candle guttered in some imperceptible draft, sending shadows crawling across the shelves. Somewhere deep in the building, a bell rang — not the evening bell, not yet, but one of the quarter-hour markers that punctuated university life with a regularity that Tomas had long since stopped hearing. He heard it now. He was aware, suddenly and uncomfortably, of time.

"You have questions," he said. "Both of you. I can see them accumulating behind your faces like water behind a dam, and if I do not manage this carefully, you will both begin talking at once and I will learn nothing. So we will proceed in order."

He turned to Bellen. He regarded the young man for a long moment — the leather satchel, the magnifying glass, the defensive posture that had not fully relaxed even now. Tomas removed his spectacles, not to clean them this time, but simply to set them aside, as though he wanted to address what came next without the mediating apparatus of his daily armor.

"Bellen. I owe you an apology."

The words came out with the stiff, reluctant precision of a mechanism that had never been designed for this particular function. Tomas's jaw worked slightly, as though the sentence had left a physical residue.

"Not for my skepticism. Skepticism is not a debt that requires repayment. I was right to demand authentication, and I will continue to demand it, and if you find that tyrannical then you have misunderstood the basic obligations of the discipline." He paused. "But I called your colleagues — scholars working on the western archive material — '*provincial contrarians with an institutional grievance.*' I wrote that. In the *Quarterly Journal.* Seven years ago. It is still in print. And I wrote it because their conclusions were inconvenient to a framework I had built my career upon, and because it was easier to attack their motives than to reckon with their evidence."

He placed his hand on the letter again.

"I had *this.* I had it in my hands when I was younger than you are now. A piece of corroborating evidence that I misfiled as an anomaly because I did not have the courage — or the imagination, I am not sure which is worse — to consider that the anomaly might be the truth and the framework might be the error. That is not a failure of method. It is a failure of character. And it is mine."

He replaced his spectacles. The familiar weight of them seemed to restore something — not comfort, exactly, but structure. The architecture of the person he had been for thirty years, cracked but still standing.

"Now. Show me what you have. Everything. The copies, your notes on the compartment, the condition of the stone, the mortar — all of it. And I want you to describe the original document as precisely as you can. Not what it *says* — I have read your transcription, and we will address the content in due course. I want to know what it *is.* The weight of the vellum. The color of the ink. Whether the seal impression was made in wax or lead. Whether the edges were cut or torn. Every physical detail you can remember, because we are going to compare your account against what we find when we examine it in person, and any discrepancy between your memory and the object itself will tell us something — either about the document or about you."

He looked at the apprentice.

"You. Journal open. Write down everything he says. Exact words. Do not paraphrase, do not summarize, do not *improve.* If he says 'brownish,' you write 'brownish,' not 'brown.' His uncertainty is data. Record it."

He turned back to Bellen and folded his hands on the desk. His expression had settled into something the apprentice had never seen — not the habitual irritation, not the grudging warmth of a reluctant teacher, but the focused, almost hungry attention of a man who had just discovered that the most important work of his life was not behind him but ahead of him, and that he might not have enough years left to finish it.

"Begin," he said.

dialogue.name: Apprentice
dialogue.content: *I quickly open my fresh journal, pen poised. I glance at Bellen, who, despite Maester Tomas's apology, still looks a bit shell-shocked. He clears his throat and begins to speak.*

S.1_consistency_over_time.rationale: Maester Tomas maintains exceptional character consistency throughout. His pedantic nature, scholarly precision, physical mannerisms (spectacle cleaning, chair complaints), and speech patterns remain constant.
